title: Arrays: Declaration, Indexing & Pointers
description: Learn how to declare, index, and traverse a 1D array in C, and how pointers relate to arrays.
congratsText: Great work! You now understand array declaration, indexing, and how pointers connect to arrays.

sections.title: Objectives
sections.lesson:
What You Will Learn
By the end of this discussion, you will be able to:
  • Declare and initialize a 1D array in C
  • Read and write individual elements using an index
  • Traverse a full array using a loop
  • Explain what a pointer is and how it relates to an array

sections.title: What Is an Array?

quiz.question: Why would you use an array instead of several separate variables?
options: To store many related values under one name, accessed by position
To make the program run without needing a compiler
To automatically sort values as they are entered
To prevent the program from ever crashing

sections.lesson:
What Is an Array?
An array is a row of numbered lockers sitting side by side in memory. Instead of creating five separate variables for five scores, one array holds all five, and each locker is reached by its number.
This matters because most real data comes in groups — a class list, a week of temperatures, a set of test scores — and an array lets one variable name represent the whole group.

sections.title: Declaring & Initializing an Array

quiz.question: What does the declaration int scores[5]; create in memory?
options: 5 separate int-sized slots, all reserved under the name scores
1 int-sized slot that can hold up to 5 values over time
A pointer named scores that has not yet been assigned
5 slots that must each be given a different data type

sections.lesson:
Declaring & Initializing an Array
To declare an array, state the type, a name, and the number of elements in square brackets.
int scores[5]; // 5 empty int slots int grades[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; // declared and filled at once
All elements in a C array must share the same data type, and the size is fixed once declared — it cannot grow or shrink later.

sections.title: Indexing: Reading and Writing Elements

quiz.question: What does scores[2] refer to in an array declared as int scores[5]?
options: The element at position 2, which is the 3rd locker in the row
The 2nd element, counting the first element as position 1
The total number of elements currently stored in the array
An error, since array names cannot be followed by brackets

sections.lesson:
Indexing: Reading and Writing Elements
Each locker has a number called an index, written in square brackets after the array name. You use it to both read and write a single element.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; printf("%d", scores[2]); // reads: prints 78 scores[2] = 100; // writes: replaces 78 with 100

sections.title: Zero-Based Indexing & the Off-by-One Bug

quiz.question: In int scores[5], what is the index of the FIRST element, and the index of the LAST?
options: First is 0, last is 4
First is 1, last is 5
First is 0, last is 5
First is 1, last is 4

sections.lesson:
Zero-Based Indexing & the Off-by-One Bug
C arrays are zero-indexed — the first locker is number 0, not 1. For an array of size 5, valid indexes are 0 through 4.
The most common beginner mistake is the off-by-one error: trying to access index 5 in a 5-element array, which does not exist and reads memory outside the array.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; // scores[5] is OUT OF BOUNDS — valid indexes are only 0-4

sections.title: Traversing an Array with a Loop

quiz.question: What does this loop print for int nums[4] = {10, 20, 30, 40};?
options: 10 20 30 40 (with an extra trailing garbage value)

sections.lesson:
Traversing an Array with a Loop
Instead of typing out every index by hand, a for loop visits each element in order — this is called traversal.
int nums[4] = {10, 20, 30, 40}; for (int i = 0; i < 4; i++) { printf("%d ", nums[i]); } // Output: 10 20 30 40
The loop condition i < 4 (not i <= 4) is what keeps the traversal safely inside the array's bounds.

sections.title: What Is a Pointer?

quiz.question: What is a pointer in C, in plain terms?
options: A variable that stores the memory address of another variable
A variable that can only hold whole numbers greater than zero
A special kind of array that resizes itself automatically
A shortcut name that C uses in place of a function

sections.lesson:
What Is a Pointer?
A pointer is a sticky note with a locker's address written on it, not the contents of the locker itself. The & operator means 'get the address of', and * means 'go to that address and look inside'.
int score = 90; int *p = &score; // p now holds the ADDRESS of score printf("%d", *p); // dereference: prints 90

sections.title: Pointers to Arrays: Array Name as Address

quiz.question: What does the array name scores itself represent, when used without an index?
options: The memory address of the array's first element
The total number of elements in the array
A copy of every value in the array joined together
An error, since array names must always have an index

sections.lesson:
Pointers to Arrays: Array Name as Address
In C, an array's name — used without brackets — decays into the address of its first element. This is why scores and &scores[0] point to the exact same place.
int scores[5] = {90, 85, 78, 92, 88}; int *p = scores; // same as &scores[0] printf("%d", *p); // prints 90, the first element
This is the deep link between arrays and pointers: an array is really just a block of memory, and its name is the address where that block begins.

sections.title: Common Pitfalls: Out-of-Bounds Access

quiz.question: Why is accessing scores[10] on a 5-element array dangerous, even if it doesn't crash immediately?
options: It reads or writes memory that belongs to something else entirely
C automatically resizes the array to fit the new index
It always prints the value 0 instead of the intended data
The compiler refuses to build the program at all

sections.lesson:
Common Pitfalls: Out-of-Bounds Access
C does not check array bounds for you. Reading or writing an index outside the declared size does not stop the program — it silently touches whatever memory happens to sit there, which can corrupt other data or crash later, seemingly at random.
Rule of thumb: for int arr[N], only indexes 0 through N-1 are safe.

sections.title: Recap

quiz.question: Which statement best summarizes the relationship between arrays and pointers in C?
options: An array's name is the address of its first element in memory
Arrays and pointers are two unrelated features that never interact
A pointer can only ever point to a single variable, never an array
Arrays automatically convert into a different data type called a pointer

sections.lesson:
Recap
An array is a row of same-type memory slots, reached by a zero-based index. Traversal with a loop visits every slot in order. A pointer stores an address, and an array's own name behaves as the address of its first element — the bridge between the two ideas.
int arr[5] = {1, 2, 3, 4, 5}; for (int i = 0; i < 5; i++) printf("%d ", arr[i]); // arr itself == &arr[0]
